resources.string: How much a company pays in dividends each year relative to its stock price.
Percentage of a company's earnings paid out as dividends each year.
The company's earnings divided by the total number of shares.
The stock price divided by its EPS.\n\nA P/E around 15 is traditionally considered a fair value.
Quarterly earnings growth (YoY).\n\nThe company's growth compared to the same quarter of the previous year.
The estimated intrinsic value calculated using the configured P/E ratio.
Expected EPS growth range for the next year.
Expected revenue growth range for the next year.
Quarterly revenue growth (YoY).\n\nThe company's revenue growth compared to the same quarter of the previous year.
Estimated years to recover the investment, assuming forward earnings growth.\n\nCalculated as: ln(1 + P×g / (EPS×(1+g))) / ln(1+g)
P/E ratio divided by the earnings growth rate.\n\nA PEG below 1 may indicate an undervalued stock relative to its growth.
The inverse of the P/E ratio (EPS ÷ price).\n\nShows the percentage of earnings generated per dollar invested.
Total cash per share divided by EPS.\n\nIndicates how many years of earnings are held in cash.
Total cash per share divided by the stock price.\n\nShows what percentage of the stock price is backed by cash.
Net income divided by shareholders' equity.\n\nMeasures how efficiently a company uses equity to generate profit.
Net income divided by revenue.\n\nIndicates how much profit a company retains from each dollar of revenue.
Total cash and equivalents divided by shares outstanding.
Total liabilities divided by shareholders' equity.\n\nMeasures a company's financial leverage.
Current assets divided by current liabilities.\n\nA ratio above 1 indicates the company can cover its short-term obligations.
Difference between the earnings yield and the 10-year Treasury yield.\n\nPositive: stocks offer more return than risk-free bonds. Negative: bonds are relatively more attractive.
Current yield of the US 10-Year Treasury bond.\n\nA risk-free benchmark rate used to evaluate investment returns.
Yield of Moody\'s Aaa-rated corporate bonds.\n\nThe highest investment-grade corporate bond yield, used as a benchmark for debt quality.
